Section heading: Our use of cookies

**Session 2022-23**

chunks.text:
Section heading: Online Safety Bill

**Further written evidence submitted by the Hacked Off Campaign (OSB64)**

**Online Safety Bill Committee**

Hacked Off’s written evidence to the Committee was critical of the criteria news publishers must meet to qualify as "recognised news publishers". Such publishers would benefit from a complete exemption for relevant social media posts & search engine results in the bill. These criteria are set out in clause 50. Our evidence cited Russia Today as an example of one publisher likely to benefit from the exemption, under the criteria as drafted.

This was disputed in one of the oral evidence sessions.

Further to that dispute please find below a note from Tamsin Allen, senior media lawyer at Bindmans LLP.

*I am a solicitor and a senior member of Bindmans LLP. I have specialised in media law for the past 23 years.*

*I have been asked by Hacked Off (of which I was formerly a director) to give my view on whether or not the media exemption to the provisions of the Online Safety Bill currently set out in Clause 50 would apply to Russia Today (assuming it was being published in the UK and was not subject to a ban for any other reason).*

*It is my view that, were the bill to become law in its present form, Russia Today would benefit from the media exemption. The exemption for print and online news publications is so wide that it would encompass virtually all news publishers with multiple contributors, an editor and some form of complaints procedure and standards code, no matter how inadequate. I understand that Russia Today is subject to a national standards code in Russia and operates a complaints procedure.*

*Moreover, this exemption could also apply to any online blog or pamphlet, even if it promoted hate, division or violence, providing it met the (minimal) requirements set out in the bill and constituted itself as a ‘news’ or ‘gossip’ publication. The only such publications which would not be exempt are those published by organisations proscribed under the Terrorism Act.*

*It seems unlikely that this is what was intended by providing an exemption for the media and I consider that the exemption requires redrafting so as to ensure that only genuine and non-harmful news publishers can benefit from the advantages of a media exemption.*

***TAMSIN ALLEN***

*Partner, Media and Information Law and Bindmans LLP*

ENDS

Submitted on behalf of the Hacked Off Campaign.

*7th June 2022*
